answers.field_label: How Long Have You Known This Trainer
answers.value: I have known Emily Cartwright for just over 9 years (since the spring of 2017). We first met when she brought a very green 5‑year‑old Mustang mare to board and train at my facility outside Loveland, Colorado, and we have stayed in close contact ever since, both professionally and personally. Over these 9 years, I have seen her progress from an eager, fairly novice colt‑starter to a very thoughtful, well‑rounded trainer who now takes on client horses, including several BLM mustangs and problem horses from the local community.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Often Do You See This Trainer Ride Or Work Horses
answers.value: During the first three years that I knew Emily (2017–2020), I saw her ride or work horses at least 4–5 days a week, often multiple sessions per day, because she boarded at my facility and also helped with exercising some of my client horses. Since she moved her own operation to a small place outside Wellington in 2021, I still see her ride or work horses in person at least once or twice a week, either when she hauls in for lessons or when I stop by her place. In addition, I regularly receive short training videos and progress updates from her—usually several times a week—so I have a very current sense of how she works. Over the course of a typical month, I would estimate I directly observe at least 10–12 of her training sessions, plus I see the results when those horses come to shows, clinics, or TIP events. I have seen her work with horses in many different contexts: initial gentling of untouched mustangs, groundwork in a round pen, first rides, arena schooling, trail riding in groups and alone, and at local open shows and obstacle challenges.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Do You Feel That Their Facilities Are Suitable For Working With A Wild Horse
answers.value:
yes – I have personally inspected and worked in Emily’s facilities, and I do feel they are suitable for working with a wild horse. She leases a 10‑acre property outside Wellington, CO, where she has set up a safe, practical, and thoughtfully organized training environment. Specifically:

- She has a solid‑sided, 50‑foot round pen constructed with heavy‑duty panels that are at least 6 feet tall, with no sharp edges or protruding hardware. The footing is level and composed of sand and fine screenings, which drain well and reduce slipping.
- There is a separate, smaller 24' x 24' starting pen attached to the round pen, built with continuous steel panels and a secure, swinging gate that can be operated from outside. This is where she initially houses and begins gentling completely untouched horses.
- Her perimeter fencing around the main turnout areas is a combination of no‑climb wire with a top rail and well‑maintained wooden posts. I walked the fence line with her last fall and did not see any broken boards, loose wire, or debris that could injure a panicky mustang.
- She has a set of safe, sturdy pens with access to shelter, where wild or green horses can be kept individually or with a calm companion. The shelters are three‑sided, oriented away from prevailing winds, with non‑slippery footing.
- Her handling equipment (halter/lead ropes, flags, long lines, lunge whips, etc.) is clean, in good repair, and appropriate for young or unhandled horses. She keeps equipment stored neatly so that nothing is left lying around where a startled horse could get tangled.

In addition to the physical setup, Emily is very strict about safety protocols: she posts clear barn rules, uses helmets for all first rides, does not allow children in the pens with wild horses, and always has a second adult on the property when she is doing anything high‑risk (like first saddling or first rides). Based on what I’ve seen, her facilities meet and in some ways exceed what I would consider the minimum standard for safely working with a wild or very green horse.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Do You Have Any Concerns About The Horses In The Care Of This Trainer
answers.value:
no – I do not have any current concerns about the horses in Emily’s care, and I have actually used her as a trainer for my own horses and client horses, which I would not do if I had doubts about her horsemanship or management practices.

Over the years, I have seen Emily handle several situations that could have gone badly in the wrong hands—such as a mustang that arrived extremely defensive about its hindquarters and a domestic gelding with a history of bolting under saddle—and in every case she prioritized the horse’s physical and mental well‑being over speed of progress or owner pressure. She is very willing to set boundaries with clients when a horse needs more time or a different approach.

Emily keeps her horses in good body condition (I would say a consistent 5–6 on the Henneke scale), with regular farrier visits every 6–8 weeks and up‑to‑date vaccinations and dental care. I know she works closely with Dr. Michael Tran at High Plains Equine Veterinary Services in Fort Collins; I’ve spoken with him about a couple of her horses, and he has also commented positively on the level of care they receive.

If I had to name a “concern,” it would be more of a personality trait: Emily is sometimes harder on herself than she needs to be. When a horse’s progress is slower than expected, she tends to blame her own program first and will call me or another mentor to ask what she could do better. I actually view this as a positive sign of humility and willingness to learn, but it does mean she sometimes takes on more emotional responsibility for difficult cases. Importantly, I have never seen her lose her temper, use excessive force, or cut corners on safety or basic care, even when working with very challenging horses or under time pressure for events.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Would You Recommend This Trainer To People With Unbroken Or Difficult Horses
answers.value:
yes – I would absolutely recommend Emily Cartwright to people with unbroken or difficult horses, and I already have on multiple occasions. In fact, several of my own clients who had horses beyond what I could take on schedule‑wise have gone to Emily with excellent outcomes.

Emily has a calm, patient demeanor that tends to diffuse tension in both horses and owners. She is particularly good at reading the small signs that a horse is getting mentally overwhelmed—tightness around the eyes, changes in breathing, small braces in the poll and rib cage—and she adjusts her approach before things escalate into a big reaction. This makes her especially well‑suited for untouched mustangs and horses with a history of fear‑based behaviors.

One specific example: in 2022, I referred a 4‑year‑old BLM mustang gelding named “Ruger” to Emily. Ruger had been adopted by a novice owner who became scared after he started striking at the front of the pen whenever anyone approached with a halter. Emily spent the first two weeks just working on approach‑and‑retreat, yielding the hindquarters from a distance, and getting him comfortable with a flag and a rope over his body. By week six, he was leading quietly, picking up all four feet, loading into a trailer, and standing tied. The owner sent me videos of Emily doing the first ride in a halter only, with Ruger licking and chewing and clearly relaxed.

I have also seen Emily handle a domestic Warmblood mare that had a history of bolting and running through the bridle. Instead of immediately resorting to stronger bits or gadgets, Emily went back to groundwork, installed a solid one‑rein stop, worked on lateral flexion, and did a lot of controlled exposure on the ground to the stimuli that tended to trigger the mare. Within three months, the mare was confidently trail‑riding in a snaffle bit.

Because Emily combines a solid understanding of groundwork, a soft but clear riding style, and a commitment to continuing education (she regularly clinics with reputable trainers like Buck Brannaman and attends local horsemanship workshops), I feel comfortable recommending her for unbroken, green, or behaviorally challenging horses. I would not hesitate to send my own personal horses, including a wild or previously mishandled one, to her program.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Personal Comments Regarding Skill Work Ethic Values Etc
answers.value:
Emily is one of the most conscientious and quietly determined trainers I have worked with in my 25+ years in the horse industry. From a skills standpoint, she has a strong foundation in groundwork and feel, and she is very intentional about building a horse’s confidence rather than simply “getting things done.” She uses a combination of pressure‑and‑release, clear boundaries, and plenty of patience. I have watched her spend an entire 45‑minute session just working on getting a nervous mustang to comfortably take a few steps toward her and then stand quietly while she rubs the horse’s neck with a flag. Many trainers would have tried to push for haltering or feet handling on day one; Emily is willing to let the horse set the pace as long as progress is steady.

Her work ethic is excellent. She is usually the first one at the barn and the last one to leave. Even in the winter, when it is below freezing and the wind is howling off the foothills, she shows up in insulated coveralls and gets the horses worked, even if that means adjusting the plan to something low‑key and safe because of the footing. I have seen her come back after dark to check on a colicky gelding and then sleep in a lawn chair outside the stall so she could monitor him overnight. She does not cut corners on basic care—waters are clean, pens are picked regularly, and she keeps detailed notes on each horse’s feed, supplements, and veterinary treatments.

In terms of values, Emily is very transparent with owners. If a horse is too much for the owner’s current skill level, she will say so, but she does it in a kind, educational way rather than making anyone feel judged. She also has a strong ethical compass when it comes to how horses are treated. I have seen her turn down a potential client who wanted a “30‑day miracle” on a severely anxious mare because she felt the timeline and expectations were not fair to the horse. She instead helped that owner find a more appropriate situation.

On a personal level, Emily is thoughtful and humble. She asks good questions, takes feedback well, and is always investing in her own learning—reading training books, watching clinics online, and riding with more advanced professionals when she can afford it. She does not pretend to know everything, which is refreshing. I trust her not only with horses, but also around youth riders. She has helped with my 4‑H group a few times, and she is patient, safety‑minded, and encouraging without sugar‑coating the realities of working with green or wild horses.

If I had to sum her up in a few words, I would say: patient, consistent, safety‑oriented, and deeply respectful of the horse. I believe she will represent the TIP Challenge program very well and provide an excellent home and training experience for any mustang placed in her care.
